---
title: "Easy Image Classification"
subtitle: "A 2-hour Masterclass · Theory, Code & Practical Demos"
author: "Sebastian Lopez-Marcano — Data Science CRP, The University of Queensland"
date: today
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, custom.scss]
    slide-number: c/t
    show-slide-number: all
    progress: true
    logo: assets/uq-mark.png
    footer: "Easy Image Classification Masterclass · UQ Data Science CRP"
    incremental: false
    transition: fade
    center: false
    highlight-style: github
    code-line-numbers: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    fig-align: center
    mermaid:
      theme: default
    title-slide-attributes:
      data-background-color: "#51247A"
      data-background-image: "assets/uq-banner.png"
      data-background-size: "cover"
      data-background-opacity: "0.18"
---

# Welcome {.title-slide}

::: {.small .muted}
A 'masterclass-style' computer vision workshop (image classification)
:::

## What you'll leave with

- A clear understanding of **computer vision** and its sub-tasks
- Confidence choosing between **classification / detection / segmentation**
- Practical understanding of **training, validation, deployment**
- Awareness of **domain shift, transfer learning, foundational models, ensembles**
- Python **code gists** you can adapt
- Short demos of **Roboflow Notebooks + Roboflow Universe**
- A direct line to the Data Sicence CRP for your own project


## Ground rules

::: {.callout-tip}
Ask as we go. 
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
Code in these slides is illustrative.
:::

::: {.callout-note}
Most of the theory will be paired with Python examples so you can see how the idea lands in practice.
:::


# 1 · Computer Vision

## What is computer vision?

::: {.cols}
::: {.col}
- Teaching computers to extract meaning from pixels
- Models learn spatial patterns inside an image (edges → textures → parts → objects)
- Modern computer vision is dominated by deep neural networks (CNNs, Vision Transformers)
:::
::: {.col}
![](assets/Computervision_banner.webp)
^[Image obtained from Algotive]
:::
:::

## Computer vision applications

![](assets/Screenshot-2025-02-13-at-11.55.59-AM.png)

^[Image obtained from [Roboflow's website](https://roboflow.com).]

## What is Roboflow
Roboflow is a platform for computer vision projects. It provides tools for:

- **Data management** — upload, annotate, version control
- **Model training** — pre-trained backbones, transfer learning, custom training loops
- **Validation and Testing** — metrics, confusion matrices, error analysis
- **Deployment** — hosted inference, API, edge deployment

https://roboflow.com/ 

## Why use Roboflow?
There are many apps and tools that are making computer vision more accessible. From experience, Roboflow is the most comprehensive and user-friendly for end-to-end projects. 

::: {.callout-warning}
Some of the advanced features (e.g. hosted inference) are paid, but the free tier is generous enough for most research projects.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
UQ or the Data Science CRP do not have any financial relationship with Roboflow. I just genuinely like their product and use it in my projects. Many people at UQ use it and have had good experiences.
:::



## The computer vision workflow
```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
    A[Define task] --> B[Data]
    B --> C[Train]
    C --> D[Val and Test]
    D --> C 
    D --> E[Deploy]
```


## Other aspects of computer vision

::: {.cols}
::: {.col}
[Data]{.badge}
High quality, labelled data is most important 'asset' in computer vision.
:::
::: {.col}
[Compute]{.badge .orange}
AI is becoming more accessible, adding more pressure into processing infrastructure. 
:::
::: {.col}
[Models]{.badge .green}
There are many pre-trained or foundation models you can already download. 
:::
:::

::: {.keybox}
You almost never need to train a model from scratch. Assume there is a pre-trained model that already does 70% of your job.
:::


# 2 · Classification vs Detection vs Segmentation

## The three core CV tasks
There are three core CV tasks that cover most use cases. They differ in the specificity of their output. 

| Task | Output | Answers | Typical use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Classification** | One (or many) labels per image | *What is in this image?* | Species ID, quality control |
| **Object detection** | Labels + bounding boxes | *What and where?* | Counting, tracking, surveillance |
| **Segmentation** | Per-pixel labels (mask) | *What, where, exact shape?* | Medical imaging, area/biomass |

::: {.callout-warning}
Cost scales with task type. Segmentation tasks are more expensive than classification tasks.
:::

---

## Visual examples

![](assets/difference-in-image-classification-semantic-segmentation-v0-apse69opd3a61.webp){width="100%"}

^[Image obtained from Reddit r/ComputerVision]

::: {.keybox}
Image classification is = Image Recognition
:::

## Visual examples (cont.)
This is how the model 'sees' the data and what it learns to output for each task. 

::: {.cols}
::: {.col .center .small}
**Classification**<br>
`{label: 'cat', conf: 0.94}`
:::
::: {.col .center .small}
**Detection**<br>
`[{label:'cat', bbox:[x1,y1,x2,y2], conf:0.91}]`
:::
::: {.col .center .small}
**Segmentation**<br>
`mask: ndarray[H,W] of class ids`
:::
:::

The `conf` scores are the model's confidence in its prediction. More information on this in the validation and testing section.

---

## Code: three tasks, three one-liners (Roboflow)
Roboflow has several python libraries to make it easy to pull pre-trained models and run inference on your images. In here we show how to use `inference`. 

```python
from inference import get_model

# --- Classification — cell type from microscopy image ---
cls   = get_model("cell-type-classifier/1").infer("slide.jpg")[0]

# --- Detection — locate nuclei with bounding boxes ---
dets  = get_model("nuclei-detection/2").infer("slide.jpg")[0]

# --- Segmentation — pixel-level cell masks via SAM 2 ---
masks = get_model("sam2/hiera_small").infer("slide.jpg")[0]
```

::: {.callout-note}
`get_model("owner/version")` pulls any model from Roboflow Universe (more info on this later)
:::

---

## Which task for which question?

::: {.keybox}
Start from the decision you want to make; work out the minimum output that enables it.
:::

| Domain | Question | Task |
|---|---|---|
| **Traffic** | *Is this vehicle a car, bus, or truck?* | **Classification** |
| **Microscopy (health)** | *How many nuclei are in this slide?* | **Detection** |
| **Agriculture** | *What fraction of the canopy is affected by disease?* | **Segmentation** |


# 3 · Collecting & Labelling Data
---
```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
    A["Define task ✅"] --> B[Data]
    B --> C[Train]
    C --> D[Val and Test]
    D --> C 
    D --> E[Deploy]
```
---

## The data-centric mindset
Data is the foundation of computer vision. A better dataset beats a better model every time.

![](assets/Image-Annotation-1.jpg){width="100%"}

^[Image obtained from DeepLobe]

## Moving away from manual labelling
In the early days of computer vision, labelling was a purely manual process. Now we can leverage **semi-automated labelling** techniques to speed up the annotation process. 

For example we can ask 'foundational models' like 'CLIP' or 'SAM' to find and label your object of interest in an image.

::: {.callout-important}
In some situations manual labelling is still necessary - for those highly domain-specific tasks where pre-trained models struggle. 
:::

## General examples of manual vs automated labelling
| Manual labelling | Semi-automated labelling |
|---|---|
| Detecting coral bleaching in a location where AI has never been trained before | Detect coral reefs and classify it into species / genus |
| Classifying plant diseases from leaf images | Segment parts of a plant (leaf, trunk, fruit etc) |



## 'Rapid' in Roboflow
Semi-automated labelling in action 

<video controls autoplay muted loop width="100%">
  <source src="assets/rapid_demo.webm" type="video/webm">
</video>

^[Video obtained from [Roboflow's website](https://roboflow.com).]


## Other cool features in Roboflow (re to data collection & labelling)
- You can upload and annotate data collected 'live' in your mobile
- Accepts images, videos, pdfs, etc 

![](assets/roboflow-upload.png){width="100%"}

^[Screenshot obtained from [Roboflow's website](https://roboflow.com).]


## Roboflow Universe: pre-labelled datasets & pre-trained models
Roboflow Universe is a public repository of datasets and models. You can search for a dataset/model that matches your problem, and either use it directly or fine-tune it on your data.

 💡Lets have a quick look at the website and see what's there - https://universe.roboflow.com/

# 3 · Training, Validation & Testing
---
```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
    A["Define task ✅"] --> B["Data✅"]
    B --> C[Train]
    C --> D[Val and Test]
    D --> C 
    D --> E[Deploy]
```
---


## The three-split doctrine
Once you have labelled data you need to split the data into three sets: Train, Validation, and Test. Each set has a specific purpose in the model development lifecycle.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    D[Labelled data] --> T[Train ~70%]
    D --> V[Val ~15%]
    D --> S[Test ~15%]
    T -->|fit weights| M[Model]
    V -->|tune hyperparams| M
    S -->|final metric| R[Final result]
```

::: {.callout-important}
The **test set is sacred**. Use it when you are ready to test / report your final results. 
:::

## How to select data for each split?
- **Random split** — simple and easy to do but problematic if there are correlations (e.g. same site, date, camera)
- **Stratified split** — ensures labelled data is balanced in each set, but difficult to apply if you have many classes
    - Training
        - 100 images for disease A
        - 100 images for disease B
    - Validation
        - 20 images for disease A
        - 20 images for disease B

## Adding variability to your splits
Models only learn from the data you show them. If your training set is too narrow, your model will fail when it sees something new in the real world.

::: {.callout-warning}
This is one of the most common sources of "it worked in the lab but fails in the field" problems. 
:::

## How to actually train a model?
There are many frameworks and libraries to train models, but here is classic object detection example

```python
from roboflow import Roboflow
from ultralytics import YOLO
import supervision as sv

# 1. pull dataset
dataset = Roboflow(api_key="YOUR_KEY") \
    .workspace("my-workspace") \
    .project("my-detection-project") \
    .version(1).download("yolov8")

# 2. train
model = YOLO("yolov8n.pt")
model.train(data=f"{dataset.location}/data.yaml", epochs=30, imgsz=640)

```

## How to interpret evaluation and testing results?
![](assets/eval-metrics.png){width="100%"}


# 4 · Domain Shift

## What is domain shift?

::: {.keybox}
**Domain shift** = when the distribution of data at deployment differs from the distribution at training.
:::



---

## Examples & quick detection

::: {.cols}
::: {.col .small}
**Examples**
- Model trained on iPhone photos → deployed on drone
- Daytime training → dawn/dusk deployment
- Great Barrier Reef corals → Red Sea corals
- Healthy crops 2024 → drought crops 2026
:::
::: {.col .small}
**How to detect it**
- Compare **feature embeddings** (e.g. CLIP features) between train and deploy sets
- Monitor **prediction confidence** distribution over time
- Track **class ratios** in production
- Periodic **spot-checking** by a human expert
:::
:::

```python
# Quick "is my new data different?" check using embeddings
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
from scipy.spatial.distance import cdist

enc = SentenceTransformer("clip-ViT-B-32")
train_emb = enc.encode(train_images)
new_emb   = enc.encode(new_images)
mean_dist = cdist(train_emb.mean(0, keepdims=True), new_emb).mean()
```

::: notes
Frame: "your metric is only as good as the assumption that tomorrow looks like yesterday." Recommend building a small drift-monitoring dashboard into any real deployment.
:::

---

## Mitigations

- **Collect a little data in the new domain** and fine-tune (usually best ROI)
- **Aggressive augmentation** that simulates the shift (lighting, blur, colour jitter)
- **Domain adaptation** techniques (DANN, CORAL) — rarely worth the complexity in practice
- **Foundation models** — large pre-trained features are more robust out-of-the-box
- **Human-in-the-loop** for the first N predictions in a new domain

::: {.callout-tip}
A **50-image fine-tune** in the new domain usually beats a cleverer domain-adaptation algorithm on the old data.
:::

::: notes
Be honest: domain adaptation is an active research area but in applied settings the pragmatic answer is almost always "label a few hundred images in the new domain and fine-tune." Tie this forward to transfer learning.
:::

# 5 · Transfer Learning

## Why it works

::: {.cols}
::: {.col}
- Early layers learn **generic features** (edges, textures)
- Late layers learn **task-specific features**
- Swap the final layer → keep the rest
- Small dataset? Only fine-tune the head. Bigger? Unfreeze more.
:::
::: {.col}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {"theme": "base", "themeVariables": {"primaryColor": "#4085c6", "primaryTextColor": "#ffffff", "primaryBorderColor": "#2d6ba8", "lineColor": "#2d6ba8", "edgeLabelBackground": "#e8f0fa"}}}%%
flowchart LR
    A[Big dataset<br/>ImageNet] --> B[Pre-trained<br/>backbone]
    B -->|freeze most layers| C[Your small dataset]
    C --> D[New classification head]
    D --> E[Fine-tuned model]
```
:::
:::

::: {.keybox}
Transfer learning turns a 100k-image problem into a 1k-image problem.
:::

::: notes
Rule of thumb: with <1k images, only train the head. 1k-10k: unfreeze last block. >10k: unfreeze everything but keep a low LR on the backbone. Mention discriminative LRs (smaller LR for earlier layers).
:::

---

## Transfer learning in 15 lines

```python
import torch, torch.nn as nn
from torchvision.models import resnet50, ResNet50_Weights

model = resnet50(weights=ResNet50_Weights.DEFAULT)

# 1) freeze the backbone
for p in model.parameters():
    p.requires_grad = False

# 2) replace the classification head (ImageNet had 1000 classes)
NUM_CLASSES = 5
model.fc = nn.Linear(model.fc.in_features, NUM_CLASSES)

# 3) only the new head is trainable
opt = torch.optim.AdamW(model.fc.parameters(), lr=1e-3)

# 4) train as usual (see Section 3)
```

::: notes
Point out: `timm` library has hundreds of backbones with the same API. Newer recipes often just use a Vision Transformer (ViT) backbone — convnets still competitive for small data.
:::

# 6 · Foundational Models

## What are they?

::: {.keybox}
Models trained on **internet-scale** data that can be used **zero-shot** or with minimal adaptation for many downstream tasks.
:::

| Model | What it does | Use it for |
|---|---|---|
| **CLIP** | Aligns image ↔ text embeddings | Zero-shot classification, retrieval, drift detection |
| **DINOv2** | Self-supervised image features | Strong backbone, embeddings, clustering |
| **SAM / SAM 2** | Promptable segmentation | Annotation acceleration, masks without training |
| **GroundingDINO** | Open-vocabulary detection | "Find anything" from a text prompt |
| **YOLO-World** | Real-time open-vocab detection | Fast detection without retraining |

::: notes
Foundational models have radically changed the workflow. 3 years ago you'd spend 2 weeks training a classifier from scratch. Now: CLIP zero-shot → SAM masks → GroundingDINO boxes → fine-tune only if needed. Mention licence gotchas (some are non-commercial).
:::

---

## Zero-shot classification with CLIP

```python
import torch, clip
from PIL import Image

device = "cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu"
model, preprocess = clip.load("ViT-B/32", device=device)

image = preprocess(Image.open("coral.jpg")).unsqueeze(0).to(device)
texts = clip.tokenize([
    "a photo of healthy coral",
    "a photo of bleached coral",
    "a photo of dead coral",
]).to(device)

with torch.no_grad():
    logits_per_image, _ = model(image, texts)
    probs = logits_per_image.softmax(dim=-1).cpu().numpy()

print(probs)   # e.g. [[0.78, 0.18, 0.04]]
```

::: {.callout-tip}
Use CLIP zero-shot as your **baseline**. If a fine-tuned model can't beat it, something is wrong.
:::

::: notes
CLIP as baseline is a habit that saves projects. Also mention: CLIP features are excellent inputs to a simple logistic regression — often matches a from-scratch CNN with 1% of the effort.
:::

---

## SAM: masks without training

```python
from segment_anything import SamAutomaticMaskGenerator, sam_model_registry

sam = sam_model_registry["vit_h"](checkpoint="sam_vit_h.pth").to("cuda")
masks = SamAutomaticMaskGenerator(sam).generate(image_rgb)
# masks is a list of dicts: {'segmentation': ndarray, 'area': int, ...}
```

::: {.callout-note}
SAM gives you **masks** but not **labels**. Pair it with CLIP or a small classifier for "mask → label".
:::

::: notes
The "SAM + CLIP" combo is the modern annotation accelerator: one click per object, automatic labels, human review. Roboflow bakes this in as "Smart Polygon" and similar tools.
:::

# 7 · Evaluation — Beyond F1

## F1 is a starting point, not the finish line

::: {.callout-warning}
A single F1 number hides **where** and **why** your model fails. Stakeholders deserve — and need — more.
:::

Questions a single F1 can't answer:

- Does it fail on the **rare** class or the **common** class?
- Is it **over-confident** when wrong?
- How does it degrade across **subgroups** (sites, seasons, camera types)?
- Are errors **safety-critical** or merely annoying?
- Is there a **threshold** I should choose differently?

::: notes
Reframe: F1 is a compressed summary — useful for model selection, terrible for deployment decisions. Push the audience to always bring at least 3 views to a stakeholder: confusion matrix, per-class metrics, calibration.
:::

---

## A richer evaluation toolkit

| Tool | What it tells you | When to use |
|---|---|---|
| **Confusion matrix** | Per-class errors & confusions | Always |
| **Per-class precision/recall** | Class imbalance effects | Imbalanced data |
| **ROC & PR curves** | Threshold trade-offs | Binary / one-vs-rest |
| **Calibration plot** | Are confidences trustworthy? | Any probabilistic output |
| **Cohen's κ / MCC** | Agreement beyond chance | Imbalanced or ordinal |
| **mAP / IoU** | Detection & segmentation quality | Detection / segmentation |
| **Subgroup analysis** | Fairness & drift hotspots | Always, if metadata exists |
| **Error case gallery** | Qualitative failure modes | Before deployment |

::: notes
Emphasise the last two: subgroup analysis and error-case galleries have saved more projects than fancy metrics. Build an "error wall" — literally a folder of the 50 worst predictions — and review it with domain experts.
:::

---

## Code: the evaluation essentials

```python
from sklearn.metrics import (classification_report, confusion_matrix,
                             roc_auc_score, cohen_kappa_score)
from sklearn.calibration import calibration_curve
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

print(classification_report(y_true, y_pred, digits=3))
print("kappa :", cohen_kappa_score(y_true, y_pred))
print("AUROC :", roc_auc_score(y_true, y_prob, multi_class="ovr"))

# Calibration plot — are the probabilities trustworthy?
frac_pos, mean_pred = calibration_curve(y_true == 1, y_prob[:, 1], n_bins=10)
plt.plot(mean_pred, frac_pos, "o-"); plt.plot([0,1],[0,1], "--")
plt.xlabel("Predicted probability"); plt.ylabel("Observed frequency")
```

::: {.callout-tip}
A **well-calibrated** 0.7 should happen ~70% of the time. If it happens 30% or 95% of the time, your confidences are lying.
:::

::: notes
Calibration is under-taught. For any system that triages or ranks, miscalibration is worse than miscoverage. Mention temperature scaling as a cheap post-hoc fix.
:::

---

## For detection & segmentation

- **IoU** (Intersection over Union) — overlap between prediction and truth
- **mAP @ 0.5** — mean Average Precision at IoU threshold 0.5 (lenient)
- **mAP @ 0.5:0.95** — averaged over 10 IoU thresholds (COCO standard, strict)
- **Per-class AP** — same story as per-class F1
- **Boundary F-score** — specifically for segmentation edge quality

::: {.callout-note}
mAP can hide systematic issues. Always **visualise predictions on ~50 images** before trusting a number.
:::

::: notes
Stress visual inspection. I have seen mAP 0.85 models that were unusable because they missed the one species that actually mattered to the ecologist.
:::

# 8 · Deployment

## What "deployment" actually means

```{mermaid}
%%{init: {"theme": "base", "themeVariables": {"primaryColor": "#4085c6", "primaryTextColor": "#ffffff", "primaryBorderColor": "#2d6ba8", "lineColor": "#2d6ba8", "edgeLabelBackground": "#e8f0fa"}}}%%
flowchart LR
    A[Trained weights] --> B[Package<br/>ONNX / TorchScript / TFLite]
    B --> C[Serve<br/>API / edge / batch]
    C --> D[Monitor<br/>drift, latency, errors]
    D -->|alerts| E[Retrain / rollback]
    E --> A
```

::: {.keybox}
**Shipping weights is 20% of deployment. The other 80% is monitoring, retraining, and the humans in the loop.**
:::

::: notes
This is the biggest gap between academic ML and production ML. Most research models never cross this line. The slide after next is the checklist you should answer before shipping.
:::

---

## The deployment checklist

::: {.cols}
::: {.col .small}
**Performance**

- Target latency (ms/frame)?
- Throughput (images/sec)?
- Batch vs streaming?

**Hardware**

- CPU / GPU / edge (Jetson, Coral)?
- Offline vs connected?
- Memory ceiling?

**Data pipeline**

- Pre/post-processing matches training?
- Input validation & logging?
- Fallback when input is bad?
:::
::: {.col .small}
**Operations**

- Versioning of model + data + code?
- Rollback plan?
- Monitoring (drift, latency, errors)?
- Retraining cadence?

**Humans & ethics**

- Who reviews flagged cases?
- Who is accountable for errors?
- Privacy / consent of imagery?
- Bias & fairness audits?

**Cost**

- $ per 1000 inferences?
- $ per retrain cycle?
:::
:::

::: notes
Walk through each bullet at pace. Highlight: "pre/post-processing matches training" is the silent killer — a 0.5 drop in mean normalisation can halve accuracy. Also: privacy of human subjects in imagery is increasingly non-optional.
:::

---

## Serving patterns

| Pattern | When | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Batch** | You have a pile of images to process overnight | Cron + script on a GPU node |
| **REST API** | Web/mobile apps need on-demand inference | FastAPI + ONNX on a cloud GPU |
| **Edge** | Network/privacy/latency constraints | Jetson Nano / Coral TPU |
| **Managed** | You don't want to babysit infrastructure | Roboflow Hosted, Replicate, Modal |

```python
# Minimal FastAPI inference service
from fastapi import FastAPI, UploadFile
from PIL import Image
import io, torch

app = FastAPI(); model = torch.jit.load("model.ts").eval()

@app.post("/predict")
async def predict(file: UploadFile):
    img = preprocess(Image.open(io.BytesIO(await file.read())))
    with torch.no_grad():
        probs = model(img.unsqueeze(0)).softmax(-1).tolist()[0]
    return {"probs": probs}
```

::: notes
For 90% of research projects, "managed" is the right answer. The time cost of self-hosting GPUs rarely pays back. Roboflow's hosted inference is a concrete option we'll see in the practical.
:::

# 9 · AI vs Expertise

## What you can *confidently* do yourself

::: {.cols}
::: {.col}
[DIY-friendly]{.badge .green}

- **Zero-shot** classification with CLIP on clear categories
- **Fine-tune** a pre-trained classifier on 500-5000 labelled images
- **Run inference** with pre-trained detection/segmentation models
- Use **Roboflow / Ultralytics / HuggingFace** end-to-end
- Simple **evaluation** dashboards (confusion matrix + curves)
:::
::: {.col}
[Call-an-expert]{.badge .red}

- Deployment at **scale** (1000s of req/sec, edge fleets)
- **Safety-critical** decisions (medical, autonomous, regulatory)
- **Novel modalities** (multi-spectral, 3D, video, time-series)
- **Custom architectures** or loss functions
- **Dataset strategy** for very rare classes / severe imbalance
- **MLOps, monitoring, drift detection** pipelines
:::
:::

::: notes
Reframe the question from "can AI do this?" to "can *I* do this with today's tools, and is the risk of being wrong acceptable?" Emphasise: the tools are genuinely great for 80% of problems. The remaining 20% eat 80% of the effort — that's where specialists earn their keep.
:::

---

## A decision sketch

```{mermaid}
%%{init: {"theme": "base", "themeVariables": {"primaryColor": "#4085c6", "primaryTextColor": "#ffffff", "primaryBorderColor": "#2d6ba8", "lineColor": "#2d6ba8", "edgeLabelBackground": "#e8f0fa"}}}%%
flowchart TD
    A[I have an image problem] --> B{Is it a standard task<br/>class/detect/segment?}
    B -- no --> X[Talk to an expert]
    B -- yes --> C{Can I collect / label<br/>a few hundred images?}
    C -- no --> Y[Zero-shot with CLIP/SAM<br/>or talk to an expert]
    C -- yes --> D{Is the consequence of a<br/>wrong prediction acceptable?}
    D -- no --> X
    D -- yes --> E[DIY with Roboflow /<br/>transfer learning]
    E --> F{Does it meet<br/>evaluation criteria?}
    F -- yes --> G[Deploy, monitor, iterate]
    F -- no --> X
```

::: notes
Keep this diagram as a take-home. Many attendees will map their project to this tree in real time. Offer to walk through it one-on-one in the DS-CRP consultation (next section).
:::

# 10 · How DS-CRP Can Help

## The Data Science Central Research Platform

The **DS-CRP** is UQ's centralised data-science support team. We exist to help researchers across the university move from *"I have data and an idea"* to *"I have a reproducible, evaluated, deployable result."*

::: {.cols}
::: {.col .small}
**We can help with**

- Framing the CV problem
- Dataset strategy & annotation plans
- Model selection & training
- Evaluation design (beyond F1!)
- Deployment advice
- Reproducibility, version control, MLOps
:::
::: {.col .small}
**How we work**

- Free short consultations
- Project scoping sessions
- Hands-on collaboration
- Training and upskilling
- Referrals to specialist partners (QCIF, RCC, eResearch)
:::
:::

::: notes
Be concrete about what a short consultation looks like: 30 minutes, bring your data and your question, leave with a plan. Emphasise that there is no charge and no commitment.
:::

---

## Book a consultation — it's free

::: {.center}
**Image classification consultation with Sebastian**

[➜ Book a short consultation](https://forms.office.com/pages/responsepage.aspx?id=z3fjtrOdy0aRovrZYFuxXHrMWx_fw6JJmXvfvvf8jRBUMk9PUVJaVjUzWFNUQkdZWkQxRDUyTTFROSQlQCN0PWcu&origin=lprLink&route=shorturl)

::: {.small .muted}
The form above will go straight to me. Bring your data, your question, or just your curiosity — I will help you figure out the next step.
:::
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
Good candidates for a consultation: *"I think I need CV but I'm not sure which task,"* *"My model works in the lab but fails in the field,"* *"I have 5000 images and no idea where to start,"* *"Should I use Roboflow or build from scratch?"*
:::

::: notes
Spend a minute on this. Explicitly invite questions from the room. If any attendee describes a project that fits, offer to book them on the spot. Also mention: I can route you to other DS-CRP specialists if your problem is tabular/NLP/etc.
:::

# 11 · Ensembles & Workflows

## Ensembles: combining multiple models

::: {.cols}
::: {.col}
**Why ensemble?**

- Different models make **different mistakes**
- Averaging reduces **variance**
- Typical gain: +1-5% accuracy, "for free"

**Types**

- **Averaging** — average softmax outputs
- **Voting** — majority class wins
- **Stacking** — a meta-model learns how to combine
- **Test-time augmentation (TTA)** — same model, multiple augmented views
:::
::: {.col}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {"theme": "base", "themeVariables": {"primaryColor": "#4085c6", "primaryTextColor": "#ffffff", "primaryBorderColor": "#2d6ba8", "lineColor": "#2d6ba8", "edgeLabelBackground": "#e8f0fa"}}}%%
flowchart LR
    I[Image] --> M1[Model A<br/>ResNet]
    I --> M2[Model B<br/>ViT]
    I --> M3[Model C<br/>EfficientNet]
    M1 --> AVG[Average / Vote]
    M2 --> AVG
    M3 --> AVG
    AVG --> P[Prediction]
```
:::
:::

::: notes
Pragmatic note: in industry, ensembles are often avoided because of latency. In research, use them shamelessly — they're a free accuracy boost. TTA is the cheapest ensemble of all (no extra training).
:::

---

## Code: averaging & TTA

```python
# --- Model averaging ---
import torch.nn.functional as F

def ensemble(models, x):
    probs = [F.softmax(m(x), dim=-1) for m in models]
    return torch.stack(probs).mean(0)

# --- Test-time augmentation ---
def tta_predict(model, x, augs):
    preds = [F.softmax(model(aug(x)), dim=-1) for aug in augs]
    return torch.stack(preds).mean(0)

augs = [
    lambda x: x,
    lambda x: torch.flip(x, dims=[-1]),   # horizontal flip
    lambda x: torch.flip(x, dims=[-2]),   # vertical flip
]
```

::: notes
TTA typically gives +0.5-2% with no retraining. Warn: keep the same augmentations at eval that make sense for the domain — flipping microscopy might be invalid.
:::

---

## Workflows: cascades & humans-in-the-loop

Ensembles combine models. **Workflows** combine *steps*. In production this often matters more.

```{mermaid}
%%{init: {"theme": "base", "themeVariables": {"primaryColor": "#4085c6", "primaryTextColor": "#ffffff", "primaryBorderColor": "#2d6ba8", "lineColor": "#2d6ba8", "edgeLabelBackground": "#e8f0fa"}}}%%
flowchart LR
    A[Image] --> B[Fast detector<br/>YOLO]
    B -->|crops| C[Accurate classifier<br/>ViT]
    C -->|low conf| H[Human review]
    C -->|high conf| O[Output + log]
    H --> O
    O --> D[Drift monitor]
    D -->|shift detected| R[Retrain trigger]
```

::: {.keybox}
A clever workflow often beats a smarter model. Think in terms of **pipelines**, not monoliths.
:::

::: notes
Real-world tip: a fast "reject" stage (is there even an object here?) + a slow accurate stage on the hits is cheaper and better than one big model on every frame. Also: a confidence-thresholded human-review stage is the single highest-leverage workflow pattern.
:::

---

## A small real-world pattern

```python
# Two-stage cascade with human-in-the-loop
from ultralytics import YOLO
detector  = YOLO("yolov8n.pt")
classifier = torch.jit.load("fine_grained_classifier.ts").eval()

def pipeline(image, conf_threshold=0.85):
    detections = detector(image)[0].boxes
    outputs = []
    for box in detections:
        crop = crop_from_box(image, box)
        probs = classifier(preprocess(crop).unsqueeze(0)).softmax(-1)
        conf, cls = probs.max(-1)
        if conf.item() < conf_threshold:
            outputs.append({"bbox": box, "label": None, "needs_review": True})
        else:
            outputs.append({"bbox": box, "label": cls.item(), "conf": conf.item()})
    return outputs
```

::: notes
Walk through: detect → crop → classify fine-grained → threshold for human review. This single pattern covers a huge fraction of applied CV projects. Roboflow Workflows (their new feature) encodes exactly this.
:::

# Practical · Roboflow

## What we'll do next (30 min)

1. **Roboflow Notebooks + Google Colab** (~15 min)
   - Open a prepared notebook
   - Train a classification model on a small dataset
   - Evaluate + download weights
2. **Roboflow Universe** (~15 min)
   - Browse public datasets & models
   - Use a pre-trained model for inference
   - Fork a dataset and adapt it to your own project

::: {.callout-tip}
Everything we do is mirrored in the companion repo: **github.com/QCIF-SustainableFutures/roboflow-training**
:::

::: notes
Housekeeping: make sure everyone has a free Roboflow account and a Google account. Share the Colab link. Remind them Colab gives a free GPU — Runtime → Change runtime type → GPU. Do a slow first pass then let them explore.
:::

---

## Roboflow Notebooks · quickstart

```python
# In Colab — first cell
!pip install roboflow ultralytics --quiet

from roboflow import Roboflow
rf = Roboflow(api_key="YOUR_KEY")
project = rf.workspace("qcif").project("my-classification-project")
dataset = project.version(1).download("folder")   # classification format

from ultralytics import YOLO
model = YOLO("yolov8n-cls.pt")
model.train(data=dataset.location, epochs=20, imgsz=224)
model.val()
```

::: {.callout-warning}
Never commit your Roboflow API key. Use Colab secrets or environment variables.
:::


---



## Roboflow Universe · quickstart

- **Universe** = public catalogue of datasets and models
- Filter by task (classification / detection / segmentation)
- **Fork** any dataset to your workspace
- One-click **deploy** for many models
- Great for **baselines** and rapid prototyping

::: {.callout-tip}
Before collecting your own data, **always check Universe first**. Someone may have already labelled exactly what you need.
:::

::: notes
Live demo: search for a dataset close to the audience's domain (marine, agriculture, medical). Show forking, show "Deploy" → "Hosted API", call the hosted API from a Colab cell. End by emphasising this is a legitimate starting point, not a shortcut.
:::

# Wrap-up

## Recap

::: {.small}
- **CV** = learning functions from pixels to structured outputs
- **Classification / Detection / Segmentation** — pick the minimum task
- **Train / Val / Test** — test set is sacred, group-aware splits matter
- **Domain shift** is the #1 real-world failure; mitigate with a little fine-tuning
- **Transfer learning** turns big problems into small ones
- **Foundational models** (CLIP / SAM / GroundingDINO) are a free head-start
- **Evaluate beyond F1** — calibration, subgroups, error galleries
- **Deployment is 80% operations**, not weights
- **Ensembles & workflows** often beat a bigger model
- **Know when to DIY vs call DS-CRP**
:::

::: notes
Keep recap fast. Invite final questions. Reiterate the consultation link and the GitHub repo.
:::

---

## Take these with you

- **Companion repo** — github.com/QCIF-SustainableFutures/roboflow-training
- **DS-CRP consultation** — [booking form](https://forms.office.com/pages/responsepage.aspx?id=z3fjtrOdy0aRovrZYFuxXHrMWx_fw6JJmXvfvvf8jRBUMk9PUVJaVjUzWFNUQkdZWkQxRDUyTTFROSQlQCN0PWcu&origin=lprLink&route=shorturl)
- **Roboflow Universe** — universe.roboflow.com
- **Papers With Code** — for any task: paperswithcode.com
- **HuggingFace Models** — huggingface.co/models

::: {.center}
## Thank you 🙏

**s.lopezmarcano@uq.edu.au**
:::

::: notes
End warm. Offer to stay behind for 10 minutes for one-on-one questions. Remind about the thumbs-up/down feedback and any survey link.
:::
